name: Paul Emery Presents
description: Mr. Emery is a professional musician, producer and audio engineer (approx. 35 years). From 1998 to 2000, he contracted with Steve Lavere to restore vintage blues and jazz recordings. He has his own audio studio and audio production equipment (used for live sound, archiving, audio restoration, album/cd production and audio editing). He also owns a van used for equipment transport. Current clients, in addition to his own productions through Paul Emery Presents, include NC Chamber of Commerce, The Center for the Arts, Strings Concerts, KVMR, Nevada Theatre, Miners Foundry, Auburn Park and Recreation District, Alasdair Fraser, North Columbia Schoolhouse Cultural Center, numerous bands, Legacy Productions, Oddfellows, GV Elks and others. In 2012, Emery was awarded the prestigious Dr. Leland and Sally Lewis Performing Arts award by the Nevada City Chamber of Commerce for his contribution and support of the performing arts in Nevada County.

name: Avanguardia Winery
description: A small (actually ultra-boutique) winery, we are the home of non-traditional blended wines, with the majority of our wines being proprietary blends crafted from 2-6 different grape varieties, many of which are Italian, and all of which are pretty much out of the mainstream. Check out our page on Our Varieties. Avanguardia does not much use the more common varietals, like Cabernet, Zinfandel, or Chardonnay. Our flagship white wine, CRISTALLO, features a grape from the former Soviet Republic of Georgia in Eastern Europe. Our most unique red, AMPIO, combines the UC-patented cross, Carmine, with the French Mondeuse for a taste sensation that is uniquely different. Our newest blend, TRE "T", after the three international varieties used (Teroldego, Tempranillo, and Tannat) is a wine of deep color and great substance, already recognized with Gold at the San Francisco Chronicle competition. Our emphasis is always on balance, with just the right amount of alcohol, acidity, tannin or oak, avoiding contemporary trends towards excess in these areas; in some ways our wines are more like Europe than California. Dare to be different and try us out---you won't be disappointed! You can visit us at the Winery, five miles due west of Nevada City, or at the Tasting Room at 163 Mill Street (pictured when you view our wine portfolio).

image.alt: Avanguardia Winery in Grass Valley
image.credit: Avanguardia Wines Tasting Room, via Google

description: BrewBilt Brewing was founded in 2021 with a deliberate focus on balanced and approachable beers made with locally sourced craft malt. This commitment to Sacramento Valley-grown barley, wheat, and oats supports local craft-minded producers like Admiral Maltings, reduces the carbon footprint of our production, and makes damn tasty beer that maintains freshness better than beer made from commodity malts. In December 2023, we opened our taproom and restaurant BrewBilt BrewHaus in downtown Nevada City. BrewHaus shares the brewery's devotion to European craft quality and local ingredients, served up by friendly faces in a relaxed atmosphere. We also have the sunniest patio in town!

description: GratiDude Ranch bridges rustic charm and modern style. It is a timeless place and once you enter, get ready for a magical experience. Originally founded in 2014 in Leipers Fork, TN., GratiDude Ranch recently opened a second location in Northern California in a charming town called Nevada City. GratiDude Ranch offers a mini-farm, private resort vacation experience. Both locations support agritourism by offering short-term overnight accommodations, private farm dinner chef experiences, murder mystery dinner events and popular learning Birthday Bash gatherings. Private and special events can also be booked. A portion of proceeds from all events help support foster children and foster families through SAFE, a 501c3 non-profit.

name: 112 W. Main St.
description: This building is known as 'The Brick Store of Silvester and Salaman' it was built in 1854 and completed in 1855 by Henry Silvester and Abraham Salaman. It is both the first and oldest original brick (and stone) building in Grass Valley and the second oldest brick building in Nevada County. There have only been three owners of this building, Henry Sylvester, Paul Wesley Michell, and Elton and Helen Williams. Only six businesses have occupied the store in 145 years, H. Silvester Grocery, H. J. Fuchs Grocery, C. W. Jenkin Variety Store, Tresize Brothers Stationery Store, Williams Brothers, later changed to Williams Stationery Store, and later became Make Local Habit.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for 112 W. Main St.

name: 400 Broad Street
description: Text of plaque: It was at this location on the afternoon of June 5, 1880 that a fire erupted in John White's upholstery shop. More than fifty buildings - including the nearby New York Hotel and most of the Chinese Quarter - were destroyed within an hour, but from the ashes of that conflagration a new business district emerged and York Street was created to ensure better downtown fire protection. Then, early in the 20th century, a doctor's office was established here. The longest-serving physician to occupy this corner was Bernard W. Hummelt, MD (1906 - 1998), who, between 1934 and his retirement in 1988, practiced internal medicine, orthopedics and general surgery. Over the course of his lengthy career he delivered over 1500 Nevada County babies. Dr. Hummelt was among the earliest physicians in the nation to administer penicillin and was instrumental in the founding of Sierra Nevada Memorial Hospital - where he served as its third Chief of Staff. Placed by the family of Bernard W. Hummelt, M.D. to honor the centennial of Dr. Hummelt's birth August 5,1906 A remarkable life- an exemplary career.

image.alt: 400 Broad Street, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: 545 Main Street
description: Druggist and County Treasurer Edward F. Spence built this house on 3 lots in 1866 and lived here with his wife Marietta until her death in 1868. He founded Spenceville, organized banks in San Jose and Southern California, served as Mayor of Los Angeles, and was instrumental in establishing the Mt. Wilson Observatory. County Judge Addison C. Niles bought the house from Spence in 1870 and occupied it with his wife Elizabeth until his election to the California Supreme Court in 1871. The City of Niles, California, is named for him. Niles Searls bought the house from his brother-in-law, Addison Niles, in 1871. When he became a Supreme Court Commissioner in 1885 he and his wife Mary gave the house to son Fred and his bride, Helen Pond, as a wedding present. In 1887 Niles Searls became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court, and served on the Supreme Court Commission from 1893 to 1899. Attorney Fred Searls (a native of Nevada City) and his wife Helen (a native of Downieville) lived here from 1885 until his death in 1929. The last member of the family to reside here was Fred Searls III, great-grandson of Niles Searls.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for 545 Main Street

description: Homesteads of Nevada County's Statesman, a 49er. Arrived here In 1850 as a journeyman printer. Editor and owner of the countyÃ

image.alt: Aaron Augustus Sargent Homestead, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Allen Chapman House
description: Allen Chapman House Built in 1856. The home and dental office of Allen Chapman, Nellie Chapman, and their son C. W. Chapman. Nellie Pooler Chapman was the first woman licensed to practice dentistry in the western United States.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Allen Chapman House
image.originalAlt: Allen Chapman House in

name: American Hill Diggings
description: On this site in 1853 Edward E. Matteson developed one of the first hydraulic mines, an operation in which gold was washed from great quantities of earth with high pressure streams of water. The method used here became a standard of the mining industry through out the world.Second plaque dedicated 2006 by Native Sons of the Golden West: The Hydraulic Monitor, Pipe & Gate Valve displayed here was installed by Native Sons of the Golden West, Hydraulic Parlor 56, Nevada City, January 2006. Note: The first approach to hydraulic mining may have been that of Antoine Chabot, said to have used water to direct gravel into the sluicebox at Buckeye Hill in 1852. But Matteson fully developed the system in 1853 by attaching a nozzle to a rawhide hose to wash down soil for gold recovery at American Hill. By the 1860s Nevada County miners universally credited Matteson with the invention of hydraulic mining and other labor-saving devices.

image.alt: American Hill Diggings, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Baruh House
description: The Baruh Home Built 1852. This site and home of two rooms was bought in 1866 by Mr. and Mrs. A. Baruh, grandparents of Mrs. Lucille Sherwood. Mrs. Clara Baruh Quigley, a charter member of Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Mr. Joseph Baruh, son, charter member of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West. Other family members: daughters Jenny Baruh Zellerbach, Rebecca Baruh Gabriel, sons Moses, Marcus and David. Mr. Aaron Baruh was a businessman operating a grocery store on Commercial Street, receiving gold dust for the commodities in the gold rush area. Later he operated a saloon and was very active in the early settlement of Nevada City. Note: Originally built by Jacob Coleman as a two-room cottage, it was expanded in 1866 by Aaron Baruh.

image.alt: Baruh House, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: The North Star House, via Google

name: Bell Hill School Bell
description: The Bell Hill School Bell 1877. This school bell is lovingly dedicated to the children and teachers of the Grass Valley School District who daily answered its melodious tone. The bell was cast at the Buckeye Bell Foundry in Cincinnati by Vanduzen and Tift, and the rotary yoke was patented in 1868. The bell was placed in this beautiful monument by Harold J. Perry, a member of the Grass Valley Host Lions Club. Dedicated March 30,1985 as a part of the 100th Anniversary Celebration of Quartz Parlor No. 58.
address: Bell Hill School, 342 S. School St

image.alt: Bell Hill School Bell, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Ben Taylor Home
description: Benjamin Franklin Taylor (1826-1913) arrived in Grass Valley on September 22, 1849, as part of a group of Mexican War veterans. He then established the Buena Vista Ranch, near the intersection of modern You Bet Road and Highway 174, where he engaged in farming and ranching. Prior to 1863, he bought a large piece of property in Grass Valley, on which in 1865-6 he built the home at what is now 652 Linden Avenue. Mr. Taylor was active in local politics and was part of the partnership that built a toll road between Grass Valley and Colfax, roughly following what is now Dog Bar Road. In 1868, he bought out some of his partners and became the principal owner. As of the time of its landmark registration, the home was still owned by Taylor's descendants.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Ben Taylor Home

name: Binkelmann Brewery
description: Born in Wurtemberg, Germany on January 2, 1828, David Binkelmann came to Grass Valley in 1853 after only a year as a new immigrant in New York. He mined for a time on Wolf Creek, then worked in a bakery. He became a renter of the business, but after two years sold the bakery operation to Herrman Maurer and bought a half-interest in a brewery with John Frank, operating in the rear of the Western Hotel which burned in 1856. The brick brewery was constructed in 1861, replacing a smaller 1856 frame building on North Auburn Street. Frank Richard bought out the partner in 1861 and was in turn bought out by Binkelmann shortly later. He ran the brewery until his death on May 23, 1894, leaving his wife Sophie to carry on until her death in 1915, when the brewery was bequeathed to her son, David J. Binkelmann. Note: The Brewery building, which has a beautiful vaulted brick cellar, has been incorporated into the hotel across the street.

image.alt: Binkelmann Brewery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Boardman House
description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.

image.alt: Boardman House, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: The North Star House, via Google

name: Calanan Park
description: Text of plaque: Native Daughters Garden in George Calanan Park. In 1922, Laurel Parlor No. 6, Native Daughters of the Golden West acquired two small lots to create a memorial park for the veterans and those who died in World War I. In the interest of 'a valuable public improvement,' Laurel Parlor sold the lot to the U.S. Forest Service for a district office in 1936. The advent of World War II redirected federal resources and the planned 6,000 square foot building was never constructed. The lot remained a community park. In 1967, the park was dedicated to honor George Humphrey Calanan (1872- 1951), a much-loved Town Trustee (City Councilman), health officer, assessor, volunteer fireman, and long time City Clerk. He was known as 'the man who got things done.' The park was granted to the City of Nevada City by the United States Department of the Interior on December 1, 1971.
address: Corner Broad and Union Sts, Nevada City. This small park is dedicated to George Calanan and contains several historical objects and plaques.

image.alt: Calanan Park, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Calanan Park Drill Core
description: Shaft Drill Core 1935-1936. Experimental vertical shaft, 5' in diameter to 1125' depth thru serpentine and gabbro at Idaho-Maryland Gold Mine, Grass Valley. Machinery invented by J. B. Newsom. Greatest drilled footage, 21' in three days. Core weight 11-tons. Donated to Nevada City by Kenneth E. Baker.

image.alt: Calanan Park Drill Core, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Calanan Park Monitor
description: The first hydraulic mining originated on American Hill near Nevada City, California in 1853. This miner's giant known as the monitor washed hillsides away under high pressure uncovering gold bearing gravel beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884 when the Sawyer decision closed hydraulic mining. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farmlands below.

image.alt: Calanan Park Monitor, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Caroline Mead Hanson House
description: Homesite of Caroline Mead Hanson. Whose idea sparked the annual Donation Day sponsored by Grass Valley Ladies Relief Society since 1883. Note: Home of the originator of Grass Valley's Donation Day, that began In 1883. Mrs. Hansen, an invalid, sat in the bay window of her home and watched the children troop to school. Hearing of the Ladies Relief SocietyÃ
address: 319 Neal St, corner of S. Church St

image.alt: Caroline Mead Hanson House, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: The North Star House, via Google

name: Charles Marsh House
description: Built about 1866 by Charles Marsh, who arrived here in July, 1849, in what would later become Nevada City. Marsh was the first County Surveyor and the first County Supervisor from Nevada City. He created the water distribution system which formed the basis for P.G.& E. and the Nevada Irrigation District. Marsh was influential in the routing of the Central Pacific Railroad and served on its original Board of Directors, as well as the Board of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. He was also instrumental in creating a Library Association in Nevada City.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Charles Marsh House
image.originalAlt: Charles Marsh House in

name: Chinatown Grass Valley (Site)
description: Chinatown, Grass Valley 1850-1938. Of the many gold rush Immigrants the Chinese were noted for their honest, sober and industrious characteristics. Each mining camp had its Chinatown and Grass Valley was second only to San FranciscoÃ
address: Corner of Tinloy and Bank Sts

image.alt: Chinatown Grass Valley (Site), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Chinese Cemetary
description: The 1852 state census recorded 3,396 Chinese living in Nevada County, about 20% of its population. Soon, anti-Chinese sentiment forced the Chinese out of the cemeteries managed by white people and into their own segregated cemetery. By the 1870s they began burial at the site of the present Chinese Cemetery. The inscription on the shrine inside the Cemetery states that the Cemetery was "reconstructed" on August 6, 1891, a date that also appears on the original gate at the entrance of the Cemetery. The site is owned presently by the Thiem family who operate the Inn Town Campground which surrounds the Cemetery, with the Chinese Historical Society having a historic preservation easement.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Chinese Cemetary

name: Chris Stevens Court
description: tevens Court honors John Christopher Stevens, United States Ambassador to Libya, who died in the service of his country September 11, 2012, in Benghazi. 'Chris' Stevens was born in Nevada County, the offspring of three generations of Grass Valleyans. Chris loved Grass Valley and spent many happy hours there. His fascination in foreign affairs and history led him to a Peace Corps stint in Morocco, followed by work in international legal law practice in San Francisco and Washington D.C. He then joined the Foreign Service, in which he served in Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Tunisia, Jerusalem and Syria. When revolution broke out in Libya, Chris was sent out on a rusty Greek freighter to report and make recommendations for U.S. policy. His work led to appointment as U.S. Ambassador to the fledgling nation. Early on he established rapport with the Libyans, as he had with the people of the Middle East and Africa wherever he was stationed with the Foreign Service. Rather than hiding behind fortified walls, he strolled the streets, talking to the people. One day after the attack that cost him his life, thousands of Libyans took to the streets protesting his death. They carried hand printed signs, some reading: 'Chris Stevens was a friend to all Libyans'.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Chris Stevens Court

name: Chuck Yeager Monument
description: A series of 5 plaques chronicles the career of Gen. Charles E. "Chuck" Yeager, a distinguished aviator, war hero and test pilot, who retired to this area. An F-104 Starfighter, the world's first Mach 2 operational aircraft which Yeager tested in 1954, is strikingly displayed behind the plaques.
address: Nevada County Airport, John Bauer Ave.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Chuck Yeager Monument

name: Colley House
description: Queen Anne Style Built in 1860 Home of Ida Colley and niece, Winifred Mulloy, founders of Laurel Parlor #6 Native Daughters of the Golden West. Now Deer Creek Inn Bed & Breakfast.

image.alt: Colley House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Crabtree House
description: Lotta Crabtree was born in New York in 1847. In 1852 the gold fever brought Mr. Crabtree to California, and a year later his wife and Lotta followed. Several months after arriving in San Francisco Mrs. Crabtree and Lotta went to Grass Valley and started a boarding house for miners with Mr. Crabtree. It was here that Lotta met Lola Montez, who taught her to sing and dance. Lotta made her first public appearance in Plumas County, which led to a successful career on stage, both here and abroad.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Crabtree House
image.originalAlt: Crabtree House in

name: Doris Foley Library of Historical Research
description: Nevada City Public Library. Library Association formed December 19, 1857. First library was in Kidd and Knox Building, 228 Broad Street. It was moved to Oustamah Lodge IOOF No. 16 June 3, 1874. Nevada City Trustees assumed responsibilities October 1, 1902 and was moved into the Corcoran Building next to the Nevada Theater. The Carnegie Endowment was given to the city in 1907. The present library was dedicated October 4, 1907. City groups raised funds for furnishings and beautified grounds 1911. Note: In 1996 the Nevada City Council, the Nevada County Board of Supervisors, and the Nevada County Historical Landmarks Commission voted to rename this library for Doris Foley, an outstanding historian and author who contributed greatly to scholarly research in this county. In addition to her writings, she helped found the Nevada County Historical Society and was the first librarian at Searls Historical Library. The Doris Foley Library of Historical Research was formally dedicated on May 17,1997.

image.alt: Doris Foley Library of Historical Research, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Dow Alexander Park
description: Formerly known as the City Square, this park was established by the Grass Valley Women's Improvement Club in 1911, as a place of rest for patrons of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. It was dedicated to the pioneers and veterans of the Spanish American War. Second plaque: Last on duty at Fort Point on San Francisco Bay. Grass Valley Women's Improvement Club arranged with the US War Department for donation to be placed in City Square, now Dow Alexander Park. Transferred from Benicia Arsenal to here in 1911. Only known example of its type to exist. Weight: 9100 pounds.

image.alt: Dow Alexander Park, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Edward Coleman House
description: The Edward Coleman House. Edward Coleman built this house in 1866. While residing here, Coleman was President/Superintendent of the Idaho Mine, Director of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad, and had interests in the Mohawk Lumber Company. Coleman sold this house in 1893.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Edward Coleman House
image.originalAlt: Edward Coleman House in

name: Elk's Lodge
description: "After constructing a second–story meeting hall above two existing 19th century commercial buildings, the Nevada City Benevolent and Protective Order of Elks #518 dedicated its first lodge at this location on June 14, 1913. The lodge hall and street–level storefronts served the community until a devastating fire broke out on the morning of March 20, 2002. Property owners Ken and Kay Baker and Gary and Patty Tintle then initiated a reconstruction program that has resulted in the current building – faithful to the architecture and style of the historic structure it replaced."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Elk's Lodge

name: Ellen Sargent
description: "Ellen Clark Sargent, a native of Massachusetts, arrived in Nevada City in 1852. A leader in the fight for women's right to vote, she founded the Nevada County Woman's Suffrage Association in 1869. She was the President of the California Woman Suffrage Association and Treasurer of the National Woman Suffrage Association. Her tireless work resulted in California women winning the right to vote in 1911. Her campaign for national women's suffrage led to the introduction in 1878 of the 19th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution by her husband, U.S. Senator A. A. Sargent. After many hard-fought battles by the Women's Suffrage Movement, the 19th Amendment was finally enacted into law on August 26, 1920."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Ellen Sargent

name: Emmanuel Episcopal Church
description: Historic Emmanuel Episcopal Church. Oldest Episcopal edifice in California. First services April 23, 1854 by Rt. Rev. William I. Kip, Bishop of Calif. This building opened for worship Aug. 1856. Note: The church was declared eligible for National Landmark status in 1982, but the vestry declined to have the church registered. The first services mentioned above were held in "a public hall" according to an account by Bishop Kip. On December 11, 1856, the Gold Hill Mining Company donated land to the parish that was bounded by Church, Walsh and Mill streets. The present church opened for worship in 1858. It is the oldest existing Episcopal Church in continuous use in California.

image.alt: Emmanuel Episcopal Church, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Empire House
description: This site at the lower end of Boston Ravine on the old main road from Grass Valley to Auburn has been the location of a public house since 1851. Charles Behrisch was the owner from 1851 to 1885. Ownership changed hands many times through the years. Purchased by Dominic Pajnich on August 1,1933, and was burned in 1934. Replaced with a 14-room hotel, named Empire Inn. Upstairs closed off in 1964, but remains a popular food and beverage stop today. Boston Ravine, established in 1849, was the earliest mining community in the area which subsequently grew into Grass Valley.

image.alt: Empire House, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: JERRYE AND ROY KLOTZ MD, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)

name: Empire Mine
description: This plaque marks the site of the Empire Mine, noted for its continuous operation 1850-1957 producing over $120,000,000 in gold.
address: Empire Mine State Historic Park, 338 E. Empire St

image.alt: Empire Mine State Historic Park in Grass Valley
image.credit: Jerrye and Roy Klotz MD, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)

anchor.label: Cultural landscape anchor
anchor.hook: A historic landscape that grounds Grass Valley arts discovery in mining history, public memory, and place.

name: Englebright House
description: Notable as one of the earliest recorded houses in Nevada City and for its history of prominent owners. The tradition that the legendary William Morris Stewart built the house in 1853 cannot be documented for no deeds remain earlier than 1858. However, the house appears as the residence of P. G. Womack in the first map of the city prepared in 1855 and published in 1856. Other owners included the famous mining engineer, Capt. Thos. Mein, whose son William Wallace Mein was born in the house in 1873. The family of the late U.S. Representative Harry Englebright purchased the house in 1897 and resided there until 1959. The interior shows the work of many occupants and the exterior reflects eclectic borrowings from many past styles.

image.alt: Englebright House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: English and New Salmon Mine Stamp Mill
description: This stamp mill was manufactured at the Miners Foundry and Supply Co., Nevada City, California, in the late 1800's. After a circuitous journey, it has returned to its place of origin. It started its industrial life of crushing hard rock at the English Mine, and then the New Salmon Mine, owned by Donald McPherson, at Badger Hill. The stamp mill ceased operation in 1942 when all non-critical gold mines were shut down. After sitting idle through the years overgrown by trees and brush the stamp mill was moved in the 1970's to the Nevada City home of King McPherson, who generously donated the stamp mill back to the Miners Foundry in the 1980's. The stamp mill sat in pieces around the foundry, despite several attempts to reconstruct it. Through the efforts of the members of Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, Native Sons of the Golden West under the supervision of Mike Nevius, restoration was completed in the spring of 2000.

image.alt: English and New Salmon Mine Stamp Mill, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: First Brick Building in Nevada City
description: Built in 1853. First brick building erected in this city. Dedicated to the memory of the pioneers of Nevada City. Note: This originally was the Dickerman carpenter shop. Bricks for this building were manufactured at Zeno P. Davis's brickyard on Lower Grass Valley Rd. in Nevada City (now called Searls Ave).

image.alt: First Brick Building in Nevada City, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: First Commercial Winery/305 Broad St.
description: "In the 1850s, over 100,000 grapevines were planted in Nevada County, often by French settlers on the western part of the San Juan Ridge. For some years, Augustine Isoard operated a wine and liquor store in this building. On June 1, 1860, he advertised in the Nevada Journal that "I have on hand a few gallons of Wine manufactured from the Grape by myself, in this city." This is believed to be the first commercial winery operation in Nevada County. After a lull in the middle of the 20th century, commercial grape growing and winemaking came roaring back. Nevada County became part of the Sierra Foothills American Viticultural Area in 1987 and home to many wineries and hundreds of acres of grapes."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for First Commercial Winery/305 Broad St.

name: Gold Quartz Discovery
description: This tablet commemorates the discovery of gold-bearing quartz and the beginning of quartz mining in California. The discovery was made on Gold Hill by George McKnight October, 1850 The occurrence of gold-bearing quartz was undoubtedly noted here and elsewhere about the same time, or previously, but the above discovery created the great excitement that started the development of quartz mining into a great industry. The Gold Hill Mine from 1850 to 1857 is credited with a total production of $4,000,000.
address: Corner of Jenkins St and Hocking Ave

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Gold Quartz Discovery
image.originalAlt: Gold Quartz Discovery in

name: Golden Era Lounge/309 Broad Street
description: Buildings on this site have housed bars and saloons since the Gold Rush era. Diana Saloon was opened in 1856. Stumpf's Miner's Hotel was established in 1863, Frank's Place (Eilerman) Saloon in 1887, and Costello & Jepson in 1910. The current building was constructed in 1886 following a major fire. W. B. Celio and H. L. Gove purchased the property in 1906, and it remained in the Celio family until 2015. Costello & Jepson established a "soda fountain" during the Prohibition years, followed by a series of bars that included the Success and Duffy's Success. Golden Era opened in 2015. The Brunswick-Balke-Collender Co.'s 'The Record' bar was built circa 1895. The front and back bars were installed in 1904 and 1915 respectively.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Golden Era Lounge/309 Broad Street

name: Grass Valley Hardware
description: Plaque dedicated 1969 by Native Daughters and Native Sons of the Golden West: Through the years of prospecting hydraulic and hardrock mining this hardware business, one of CaliforniaÃ

image.alt: Grass Valley Hardware, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley Historical District
description: Dedicated in 1969 by the Grass Valley City Planning Commission. Description: A colorful, old gold-mining town established in 1849 at Boston Ravine and later named Grass Valley by a company of emigrants. The atmosphere of early mining days still clings to the town. Significant sites include the Empire Mine State Historic Park, the North Star House and the homes of Lola Montez and Lotta Crabtree.

image.alt: Grass Valley Historical District, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley Masonic Lodge
description: Masonry in Grass Valley. Madison Lodge #23 was established under dispensation from the Grand Lodge of California on May 4th 1852. Its permanent charter was issued on May 5th 1853. Madison Lodge held its first two meetings at the old Columbus School on the present site of Hennessy School. The Lodge moved to a building on the south side of Main St. where it now meets Auburn St. This wooden building was destroyed along with most of Grass Valley in the great fire of September 12 and 13, 1855. A few days later, the Masons met with the Odd Fellows to select a site for a new brick building to house both organizations at 110 Mill St. This building was Madison's home for nearly 73 years, until fire destroyed the Masonic portion of the building in December 1928. For the next nine years the Lodge met in the auditorium of the old Strand Theater at 161 Mill St. During this time, planning was being completed for our present building, which saw its first meeting on January 29, 1938. For 150-years, in both good times and bad, through 7 wars and a great depression, the Masonic spirit of Brotherly Love, Relief and Truth has continued on through our brethren of honorable men of all backgrounds, professions and faith. This plaque was set on May 9, 2002 in rededication ceremonies conducted by C. Ray Whitaker, Grand Master of Masons in California and Elmer B. Curtis, Master of Madison Lodge.

image.alt: Grass Valley Masonic Lodge, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley Public Library (Josiah Royce Library)
description: There are four plaques. First plaque and fir tree dedicated 1931 by Native Daughters and Native Sons of the Golden West: 1854 - Himalayan Fir - 1916. Planted In Memory of Dr. Josiah Royce one of AmericaÃ

image.alt: Grass Valley Public Library (Josiah Royce Library), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Grass Valley's Famed Maple
description: This beautiful, Brandywine Maple, subject for artists and photographers, was brought from the fields of Gettysburg, and planted in 1876 by the widow of the poet and editor of the Grass Valley National newspaper, John Rollin Ridge. Surviving the fire which leveled the home, it now adorns the lovely Victorian home built by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Edward Clinch in 1900. Roy Clinch Tremoureux, grandson, notes proudly that many trees which beautify our town have come from this famed maple. Note: Time has ravaged the maple.
address: Neal St, in private yard at corner of School St

image.alt: Grass Valley's Famed Maple, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Hirschman's Pond
description: "Nevada County's diverse gold rush population included a large Jewish community. Most were immigrants from Europe, attracted by the opportunity to make their fortunes and by the County's relative freedom and absence of discrimination against Jews. Many were merchants and many were miners. One of the more successful miners was Leb Hirschman (1823-1893), a native of Bavaria, who arrived here in the early 1850s, and mined around Oustamah Hill. In 1866, Hirschman, Grover & Co. purchased the American Hill Diggins, one of the the principal hydraulic mines in Nevada City, and operated it for many years. This pond, on the site of the mine, bears his name."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Hirschman's Pond

name: Holbrooke Hotel
description: Holbrooke Hotel Founded 1851. California's oldest hotel in continuous operation. Among its century-old archives are names of such notables as Presidents Grant, Harrison, Cleveland; authors Mark Twain, Bret Harte; boxers Corbett, Fitzsimmons; actor Gilbert Barry, and highwayman Black Bart. Present structure built in 1862 following infamous fire. Landmark registered and second plaque dedicated 1978 by State of California, CHL 914: Ã±The present bar of the hotel has been in continuous operation since 1852, when it was known as the Golden Gate Saloon. The wooden building had a rear extension called The Exchange Hotel and offered food and lodging by January 1853. The saloon was destroyed in the fire of 1855 and rebuilt out of fieldstone with a brick front. The Holbrooke, built in 1862, had the adjacent Golden Gate Saloon incorporated in the hotel building. Third plaque dedicated by Grass Valley Downtown Association: Presidential Square. These Presidents of the United States have visited this site: Ulysses S. Grant, James A. Garfield, Benjamin Harrison, Grover Cleveland, Herbert Hoover. Note: No evidence exists that some of these notables stayed at the Holbrooke or visited Grass Valley. Local lore has it that Gilbert Barry is a Clamper fabrication.

image.alt: Holbrooke Hotel, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Frank Schulenburg, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 4.0)

name: Hydraulic Mining
description: The first hydraulic mining originated here at American Hill Diggins by Edward Matteson in 1853. Matteson originally used canvas hose. The canvas hose was replaced with crinoline hose in 1860. These miners' giants, also known as water cannons, monitors and dictators, washed hillsides away with high pressure water, uncovering gold bearing beds. This mining technique flourished profitably until 1884. Rivers were being filled with mud and debris causing flooding of the rich farm lands in the valley. At that time, a lawsuit brought by farmers against hydraulic mining was heard in Marysville, California where a decision known as The Sawyer Decision stopped all mining. Under a special use permit, granted by the Department of Fish and Game, these hydraulic monitors were last used at the Pacific Slab Mine near Foresthill, California in 1970, which was documented by the California Department of Parks and Recreation.
address: Railroad Ave at back entrance to Northern Queen Inn

image.alt: Hydraulic Mining, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Lawrence & Houseworth, publisher, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Idaho Maryland Core Sample
description: Plaque dedicated 1961 by Historical Society: Core - Weight 11 Tons. Portion taken from Idaho Shaft No. 2 bored to a vertical depth of 1,125 feet, diameter of shaft five feet, the core boring barrel was invention of J. B. Newsom, mining engineer. Was used as an experiment by the Idaho Maryland Mine Corporation under general manager Errol MacBoyle. With the cooperation of the directors of the 17th District Agricultural Association, through their manager, Malcolm E. Hammill, this core was erected in July 1961. Note: The Newsom drilling machine bored a round shaft, using no explosives. It was cheaper because no timbering was needed. Similar cores are displayed at Calanan Park and Empire Mine Park.

image.alt: Idaho Maryland Core Sample, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Idaho-Maryland Mine Site
description: The Idaho quartz mine was discovered in 1863. It prospered under the Coleman brothers from 1867-93, then consolidated with the Maryland and other small mines. Eventually it became one of the 'Big Three' of the Northern Mines, the other two being the Empire and the North Star. Its final years under the management of Errol MacBoyle were very productive. He developed a revolutionary method of shaft sinking, using a huge core drill called the Newsom drilling machine. World War II and government order L208 brought about final closing. Note: Various parts of the mine still exist. The office building is off the east side of Centennial Drive, between Idaho Maryland Road and Whispering Pines Lane. A headframe is located near the Grass Valley Scrap, Iron & Metal yard. Learn more about the mine from Gold in Quartz: The Legendary Idaho Maryland Mine by Jack Clark, or from MacBoyle's Gold by Gage McKinney, both available from Comstock Bonanza Press.
address: Hillside adjacent to Centennial Dr and Whispering Pines Lane, south of Idaho Maryland Rd

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Idaho-Maryland Mine Site
image.originalAlt: Idaho-Maryland Mine Site in

name: Indian Medicine Stone
description: Pioneer Emigrant Trail. This boulder was known to the pioneers as the Indian Medicine Stone. On its top are hollows in which the Indians lay while taking sun baths to cure their ills. One branch of the Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento passed close beside this stone.
address: 621 E. Broad St, opposite N. Pine St

image.alt: Indian Medicine Stone, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed
description: The Wagon Shed. Generations of Californians living in the northern mines depended for their economics and social well-being on wheel transportation powered by the horse, mule, the ox. The vehicles displayed here represent some of those used to move people, goods and gold. The Nevada County Historical Society, Hydraulic Parlor No. 56, City of Nevada City, California Department of Parks & Recreation, Nevada City Lions Club and many other civic groups and individuals cooperated to preserve this collection. Second plaque dedicated 1993 by the Historical Society: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed. This building is dedicated to the memory of Isabel Ross Hefelfinger (1899-1991). She encouraged many by her interest and dedication in remembering the past. Her untiring efforts to preserve history of Nevada County will not be forgotten.

image.alt: Isabel Hefelfinger Wagon Shed, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Kentucky Flat School
description: he Kentucky Flat School was originally located near the Kentucky Flat mining camp below Kentucky Ridge along the old road to Bridgeport, today's Bitney Springs Road. Within a few years the mining camp's gold had been depleted and its residents moved on. In 1855, the Kentucky Flat School was moved to its present location at Newtown, two miles east of Kentucky Flat. The towns were two of the earliest established gold mining camps in Nevada County, both founded in 1850. Mr. Hubertus Schardin, who emigrated from Germany, named Newtown and is considered its founder. He established a store and market there in 1850 to supply the miners. On August 21, 1877 Mr. Schardin deeded to the Kentucky Flat School District the property the school now sits on. Tragically, in 1895 the original Kentucky Flat School was destroyed by fire. It was quickly rebuilt the same year. The School continued to operate for 108 years, until 1963 when it was closed after proudly serving generations of Nevada County school children. The School was annexed to the Grass Valley School District in July 1964 and leased to the Kentucky Flat Community Center. Today, in 2022 it continues to serve young children from surrounding areas as the Kentucky Flat Community Pre-school

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Kentucky Flat School

name: Kentucky Ridge Mine Site
description: During gold rush days, Nevada County had a significant Black population. Most were free, but even though California had entered the Union as a free state, some were enslaved before the Civil War. From 1851-1852, the Kentucky Ridge Mine was the largest enterprise in California operated by slave labor. Gold was first discovered on this property in early 1851. By October of 1851, Colonel William F. English brought with him from Florida and South Carolina about 40 slaves to operate the gold mine. He was one of over a dozen slave owners in Nevada County. The mine failed after English died in a gun accident on August 27, 1852, and the Black mine workers gradually moved into the towns. One of them, Isaac Sanks, the husband of Chasey, one of the slaves, became a religious leader, businessman and political figure who helped gain Blacks the right to serve on juries and the right to vote by 1870. He died at his home in Grass Valley on May 9, 1894

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Kentucky Ridge Mine Site

name: Lone Grave
description: Julius Albert Apperson Born June 1855-Died May 6,1858. A pioneer who crossed the plains to California who died and was buried here. The Emigrant Trail followed along this ridge and through Nevada City. The marking of this lone grave perpetuates the memory of all lone graves throughout the state of California. Plaque incorrectly identifies this Landmark as NEV 001. Note: Julius Apperson was not a passerby. The boy died as the result of burns sustained on his parents' ranch and he was buried on Apperson property. The original date on the grave was May 31,1858. There are two white wooden grave markers nailed to trees behind the fenced-in gravesite. One says "In memory of Julius Albert Apperson, died May 6, 1858, age 2 years, 2 mos., 25 days." The other contains the boy's initials and dates. A gravestone on the ground inside the fence reads: In Loving Memory of Julius Albert Apperson, Died May 6, 1858, Age 2 yrs, 2 mos, 25 days." However, if that was his true age he would have been born in February 1856 instead of June 1855, as the plaque states. References; (1) Testimony of John Milton Apperson, a Tehama County cattleman and older brother of Julius, on February 25, 1936; (2) "Lone Graves of Nevada County" in the Nevada County Historical Society Bulletin, January 1961; (3) an item in the Nevada Democrat, May 5, 1858, stating that the child was terribly burned near the house where he lived on April 30.
address: Hwy 20, 8 m. east of Nevada City

name: Loutzenheiser Pharmacy Building/Foothill Flowers
description: Operated by pioneer druggist William Loutzenheiser (1824_1905) and son, John G. (1861-1929). The pharmacy begin here in a wooden building in 1851. The structure gave way to the present brick edifice in the fall of 1855. William was a member of the 1861 city Board of Trustees, one of the original locators of the Idaho Quartz claim, and was active in several other mining ventures. On July 4, 1887 the pharmacy was the site of a bloody duel between two Grass Valley merchants, Henry Sylvester and John Davis, in which the latter was killed. In 1928 the business changed hands after 77 years of continuous operation by the Loutzenheiser family. Between 1928 and 1966 the building had many tenants. In the fall of 1966 Marie Johnson, known as the Flower Lady, founded Foothill Flowers. The interior of the building was destroyed by fire in May 1984 and was subsequently remodeled.

image.alt: Loutzenheiser Pharmacy Building/Foothill Flowers, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Martin Luther Marsh House
description: Martin Luther Marsh arrived in Nevada City in 1851 after a journey from Ohio by the Nicaragua route. He worked as a carpenter, left to mine at Iowa Hill and finally returned to enter the lumber business. With his brother Dan he set up a saw mill on Little Deer Creek (now Pioneer Park). The enterprise boomed and in 1873 Marsh built this magnificent home using carefully seasoned sugar pine for the interior woodwork. His granddaughter Lucille and her late husband James B. Christie restored the house in 1955.

name: Memorial Grove Nevada City
description: This grove honors those who served our country in the armed forces. It contains five plaques. Plaque dedicated 1946 by the citizens of Nevada City: This plot of land set aside by the City of Nevada, these trees living tributes to our war dead. This plaque and flag pole erected by the citizens of Nevada City dedicated July 4, 1946 to the memory of our heroes who gave their lives in World War II that the American way of life may continue. Caswell Fisher, Tevis Ferguson, Merton Downey, Donald Osrey, Bert Graham, Fred Doby, Eugene Davies, Albert Conti, Ernest Chapman, William G. Bennett, Hans G. Hirsef, Clifford M. Ruth, Timothy R. Livesay, Virgil Roseborough, David McCreary, Eugene Rickard, Robert D. Proctor, Louis G. Netz, Glenn Ponticelli , Walter O. St. John, Carl D. Tobiassen, Gus Wasley, James M. Odegaard, William Pohley, Reid Shurtleff. Plaque dedicated 1990 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Korea, June 25, 1950 - July 27, 1953, 'The Forgotten War - Remembered.' Let us not forget those Americans who served, those who died and those who still remain on that foreign soil in defense of freedom. As a result of that war: 54,246 Americans were killed 8,177 Americans are missing in action 389 Americans are prisoners of war. Plaque dedicated 1992 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: Vietnam. All gave some - some gave all. If you are able, save for them a place inside of you and save one, backward glance when you are leaving for the places they can no longer, go. Be not ashamed to say you loved them, though you may or may not, have always. Take what they have left and what they have taught you, with their dying and keep it with your own. And in that time when men, decide and feel safe to call the war insane, take one moment to, embrace those gentle heroes you left behind. - Major Michael Davis O'Donnell, 1 January 1970, Dak To, Vietnam. Note: Major O'Donnell was a helicopter pilot. On 24 March 1970, while attempting to rescue eight soldiers, his chopper was shot down. He and, three crew members were declared missing in action. This memorial plaque is dedicated as a tribute to those who served and fought in the Vietnam War, especially those who died, and those yet to return. Plaque dedicated 1995 by Banner Mountain Post No. 2655, Veterans of Foreign Wars: POW MIA You are not forgotten. Plaque dedicated 2007 by the citizens of Nevada County: War on Terror We Will not Forget. In honor of the Men and Women who have served and will serve in the ongoing Global War on Terror and to those Valiant Few who have given their lives, therein... . We, the citizens of Nevada County, dedicate this memorial. L/Cpl Adam Strain, 20, L/Cpl John 'J.T.' Lucente, 19, 1st Lt. Nathan Krisoff, 25, Cpl Sean Stokes, 24, Ssg David P. Senft, 27. There are also memorial plaques for the following Vietnam veterans located on the following Golden Center Freeway overpasses around Nevada City. PfcUSA: Philip A. Tritsch 06/10/1943-01/28/1969, west end Washington St SP/4 101st ABN: Ronald J. Walber 06/3/1948-04/25/1968, west end Broad St Michael D. Goeller 06/2/1948-06/01/1969, east end Sacramento St Capt USA: John Stuart Seeley 04/10/1932-06/27/1966, west end Gold Flat Rd 1st Lt USA: James F

name: Naffziger House
description: Boyhood Home of Howard Christian Naffziger. 1884 M.D., F.A.C.S. 1961. World-renowned neurosurgeon. Professor of surgery, Chief of Surgery and Neurosurgery at University of California Medical School; and Regent of the University of California. House built in 1859 by J. H. Helms. Note: James Harvey Helm (not "Helms") was a City Trustee and operated the Empire Livery Stable on Broad Street.

name: Nathaniel P. Brown House
description: Nathaniel P. Brown, founding owner of the Nevada Daily Transcript and early political figure in Nevada City, lived here from 1865 until 1909. He expanded the original cottage to its current size in the 1880s, allowing for frequent entertaining. The home was built in 1858 by Jesse S. Wall, an early settler and merchant. Nathaniel Brown came to Nevada City from Massachusetts in 1853 at the age of 18, following his friend Aaron Sargent. He was part owner of the weekly Nevada Journal from 1855 to 1859. In 1860, Brown founded the countyÃ

name: National Exchange Hotel
description: Originally the Bicknell Block, stagecoach, mail, express and telegraph center in mining days. Companies which became the Pacific Gas & Electric formed here. Site of first whipping post in state. Facade unchanged since 1854. This hotel, one of the oldest in continuous operation west of the Rockies, opened for business in August, 1856. Originally known as the 'Bicknell Block,' the structure consists of three common-walled buildings of simple brick construction embellished with classic revival and wrought iron railing ornamentation.

name: Nevada Brewery
description: The Old Brewery. Constructed of granite blocks from the Sierras about 1882. Where Simon C. Hieronimus & family brewed and served lager beer to Nevada City, Queen of the Northern Mines and hydraulic mining communities. Note: This brewery was erected on the site of Temperance Hall after that building burned. After his brewery on Searls Avenue (See 102) burned in 1893, Simon Hieronimus brewed his beer in this building until he died in 1911.

image.alt: Nevada Brewery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Nevada City AME Church Site
description: "On this site in 1864, the African Methodist Episcopal Church was dedicated by Bishop T.M.D. Ward to serve Nevada City's Black community, many of whom lived nearby and had been attending services in Grass Valley at the AME church established there in 1854. The church's founders were Dennis Drummond Carter, Samuel Rodgers, Elijah Booth, John Hamilton, and James Jenkins. The original building was destroyed in a storm in 1865, but was soon rebuilt by the community. In addition to holding religious services, the church was a center for social, cultural, political and civil rights events, such as the "festivals of freedom" that commemorated the anniversary of the Emancipation Proclamation. The building also housed a "Sabbath School" established in 1867. The church was used until 1894, by which time the membership had dwindled and the property was sold and became a series of private residences. The proceeds from the sale of the property were returned to the AME Church."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Nevada City AME Church Site

name: Nevada City Firehouse No. 1
description: Nevada Hose Co. No. 1 Occupied this firehouse from May 30, 1861 to 1938. Hand pulled hose carts, horse drawn engines and motorized pumpers all rolled out of this building to save life and property. Note: The building now houses the Historical Society Museum.

image.alt: Nevada City Firehouse No. 1, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Nevada City Firehouse No. 2
description: Firehouse #2 or Pennsylvania Engine Company #2 January 14,1861. Fire company formed June 13,1860 as Eureka Hose No. 2 with 34 volunteers. Name changed in August of that year to 'Pennsylvania Engine.' Construction money raised in 1859 by popular subscription and a grand ball staged by the townÃ

name: Nevada City Hall
description: First plaque dedicated 1937 by Native Sons of the Golden West: Dedicated June 19,1937 to the Pioneer Councilmen who erected the first City Hall Felix Gillet, K. Casper, J. C. Locklin, Alec. Gault, Dan. Baker and to H. D. Shearer and Wm. H. Kilroy Officers who were killed in line of duty.... Second plaque (on floor of porch): This building dedicated to Truth-Liberty-Toleration by the Native Sons of the Golden West June 19,1937.Third plaque dedicated 1987 by Native Sons of the Golden West: U. S. Constitution. 1787 Bicentennial Nevada City. A Bicentennial Community. Fourth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On February 14, 2000 a contract was executed, authorizing the completion of work that began in 1937. On that date, the City Council inaugurated a process by which a small, historically significant City Hall was transformed into a spacious and efficient public facility. Expansion and renovation have been fruitful to the original architectural style and designed to serve the community far in to the 21st century.... Dedicated to all who came before us and all who will follow. Fifth plaque dedicated 2000 by Nevada City Council: On the occasion of the dedication of a new City Hall at this location on December 19, 2000, the citizens and City Council of Nevada City humbly submit for posterity a time capsule secured in a concrete vault beneath this marker. Contained within are artifacts and documents representative of life in Nevada City as we prepare to embrace the uncharted future. We respectfully request this repository remain undisturbed until April 17, 2150, at which time Nevada City will celebrate its 300th birthday.

name: Nevada City High School Site
description: Plaque dedicated by former students: Nevada City High School was located on this site from 1912-1952. This plaque was placed by former students as a lasting memorial to their high school. Note: After merger of Grass Valley and Nevada City high schools, Seven Hills School used the building as a junior high school. In 1973 the buildings were sold to the County of Nevada and eventually torn down. A charter school occupies the adjoining grounds.
address: Near sidewalk, across from 626 Zion St

name: Nevada City Jewish Cemetery
description: The Nevada City Jewish Cemetery was established in 1854. The first burial was in 1856 and the last in 1890. Twenty nine headstones are visible, including that of Louis W. Dreyfuss, a cousin of Captain Alfred Dreyfus of France, the victim in the infamous Dreyfus Affair. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism. Note: For more information see A Traveler's Guide to Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries of the California Gold Rush by Susan Morris.
address: End of King Hiram Lane, off Searls Ave

image.alt: Nevada City Jewish Cemetery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Nevada City Masonic Lodge
description: The year was 1850 at a location not far from here. The first Masonic Lodge meeting was held under a Charter from state of Wisconsin. Lafayette Lodge #29 was born, the years following in 1851 & 1863 a major part of which was to be known as Nevada City was destroyed by fires. By this time California was a State and a new Lodge of California Charter was established as Nevada #13 F&AM in 1851. At this location the first masonry structure was built in 1852 as a store. The 1863 fire destroyed this building and again the Lodge at a different place. This site was purchased for $1,650 and rebuilt as a store with Lodge rooms up stairs. The Lodge moved in 1864. A dining room/office extension was built to the right in 1901. Over the past 136 years other Masonic units/orders have also been meeting here. Royal Arch, Nevada Chapter #6, Council #62, K. T Commandery #6, Evangeline Eastern Star #9. In honor of 150 years continuous Nevada Lodge #13 F&AM Masonic Actives. Grand Master of California Alvin J. Weis placed this plaque in rededication of this Lodge April 22, 2000. Note: The plaque is quoted verbatim; all errors are in the original.

description: Historic Nevada City Methodist Church. Dedicated in 1864. Now one of the oldest existing churches.

name: Nevada County Court House
description: Nevada County Courthouse History. Nevada County organized out of Yuba County May 11,1851. Wooden court-house and log jail built on Broad Street in 1851. The first courthouse on this site was erected in 1856. The building was damaged by fire in July of the same year and rebuilt. Destroyed by fire in 1863 and rebuilt in 1864. Remodeled and enlarged in 1900. Modernized and enlarged 1936-37, George C. Sellon, Architect, Lindgren and Swinerton, Inc., Contractors, Robert Steger, Building Superintendent.
address: 201 Church St, on left side of entrance

image.alt: Nevada County Court House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge (III)
description: For 66 years, the Nevada City depot served as the northern terminus for the railroad affectionately known as the 'Never Come Never Go.' In 1874 a group of 7 men, led by lumber mill and gold mine owner John C. Coleman, organized the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad. Engineer John Flint Kidder was hired on January 14, 1875 to build a 23-mile narrow gauge railroad from the Central Pacific Railroad connection in Colfax to Grass Valley and Nevada City. On a snowy May 20, 1876, John Coleman drove the last spike in Nevada City to dedicate the completion of the railroad. John Kidder controlled the railroad from January 1884, until his death on April 10, 1901; the following day, his widow Sara was elected president of the railroad. Sara Kidder successfully managed the company until its sale on April 24, 1913 to the Oakland, Antioch and Eastern, a predecessor to the Western Pacific. On May 13, 1926, the N.C.N.G.R.R. was purchased by a group of local investors and it operated until December 1942. The scrapping of the railroad was completed by May 5,1943. Two other plaques at this location were dedicated on June 11,1994, but cannot presently be located. Text of second plaque: In fond appreciation of the known surviving employees of the Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad Co. 1876-1942. Bob Paine, Tony Dellea, Sadie Angelini, Eugene Katzer, Homer Bevared, George Jefferson, Edward Smith, Bill Tremewan. Text of third plaque: In grateful appreciation to the Northern Queen Inn and the Ramey family for their dedicated efforts in the restoration and preservation of Nevada County's railroad history.
address: Railroad Ave at back entrance to Northern Queen Inn

image.alt: Nevada County Narrow Gauge (III), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge RR (II)
description: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad was dedicated May 20, 1876 one hundred feet east of this spot. Built by John Flint Kidder between 1874-76 it ran 22 miles from Nevada City to Colfax via Grass Valley. Rails taken up in 1942. Sarah Kidder, widow of builder, worldÃ

name: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad (I)
description: Nevada County Narrow Gauge Railroad ran from here 5 miles N to Nevada City and 17 miles S to Colfax. Built 1874-76 by John F. Kidder whose home is NE corner. Depot and shops were 50 yards N., rails removed 1942. Sarah, widow of builder, was first woman R.R. president.
address: Entrance at Bennett and Bank Sts. Plaque at Bennett and Kidder Sts.

name: Nevada County Traction Company
description: Nevada County Traction Company 1901-1924. Our 28-ton standard gauge street cars ran from this spot five miles to Boston Ravine south of Grass Valley. Car barns and power plant located at Glenbrook mid-way. Line was victim of motor cars and busses.
address: Corner of Pine and Broad Sts

description: A historic Nevada City performance house with a long cultural memory, still useful as a recognizable arts-night anchor.

name: North Star House
description: This 1905 structure was built for Arthur D. Foote, manager of the North Star mine from 1895 to 1913, and his wife, Mary Hallock Foote, a noted 19th-century author and illustrator. The structure was designed by Julia Morgan, California's first licensed female architect and designer of the Hearst properties at San Simeon, Asilomar in Pacific Grove, and numerous buildings on the U. C. Berkeley campus, and in total, over 700 buildings. The property is representative of the era that brought Nevada County to the forefront of gold production in California.

image.alt: North Star House in Grass Valley

anchor.label: Heritage design anchor
anchor.hook: A Julia Morgan-designed landmark where architecture, mining history, restoration, and civic imagination come into view.

name: North Star Mine Powerhouse and Nevada County Traction Company
description: The North Star Powerhouse, built by A. D. Foote in 1895, was the first complete water powered compressed air transmission plant of its kind. Compressed air, generated by Pelton water wheels, furnished power for the entire mine operation. The 30-foot Pelton wheel was the largest in the world, and was in continuous use for over 30 years. Second plaque dedicated 1991 by American Society of Mechanical Engineers: International Mechanical Engineering Heritage Collection Pelton Water Wheel Collection North Star Power House Museum. The Pelton Wheel uses the momentum of a water jet impinging on buckets attached to the periphery of a wheel to produce power. It is a development of the primitive, so-called 'Hurdy Gurdy' wheel used in the California goldfields in the nineteenth century. Various inventors have contributed this type of water turbine, including Lester A. Pelton (1831-1908), who in 1880 patented the characteristic split bucket form. Subsequent important contributions to Pelton-Wheel technology were made by W. A. Doble. This collection illustrates the origin and development of the Pelton Waterwheel. American Society of Mechanical Engineers-1991.Third plaque dedicated 1997 by Historical Society near smaller wheel: Pelton Type Water Wheel. Produced 71000 hp at 327 rpm, diameter 10 ft. Weight 26,000 lbs. Built by Allis Chalmers of single cast nickel steel. Gift of Oroville-Wyandotte Irrigation Dist. Note: The building presently houses the Historical Society's Mining Museum.
address: Allison Ranch Rd at McCourtney Rd

name: Old Brick Gothic Church Building
description: Historic Old Brick Gothic Building. Constructed as a Congregational Church in 1857. Restored by First Baptist Church. Notes: Also site of Nevada City's pioneer nondenominational church, which burned in March 1851. It was replaced by a wooden Congregational Church, that burned in 1856. Cornerstone of this brick building was laid on July 4, 1858 (not 1857.) The roof and interior burned in 1863 and the church was rebuilt once more in 1864. The University of California was born at the church in 1853, when Henry Durant took part in a convention of Congregationalists and Presbyterians who resolved to establish a college in California. Durant soon opened the Contra Costa Academy in Oakland, that become the College of California then relocated to Berkeley as the University of California.
address: Corner of Main and Church Sts

image.alt: Old Brick Gothic Church Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Pine Street Crossing at Nevada City
description: Pine Street Bridge 1996. Third span of Deer Creek at this location. It is a replica of the single arch steel Gault bridge (1903-1996) which replaced the suspension bridge (1862-1903) built by Andrew S. Hallidie, who later built the San Francisco cable car system. Note: The bridge is officially named the South Pine Street Bridge at Hallidie Crossing. Hallidie's patented structure collapsed two weeks after it opened, killing two men. The Gault bridge, similar to the one at Edwards Crossing (No. 128) was named for Nevada City Mayor Alexander Gault.
address: S. Pine St at Deer Creek

name: Pioneer Cemetery
description: This cemetery was established in 1851 on a knoll back of the First Methodist Church. This gave the site its name. Many noted citizens rest here. Note: The first cemetery in Nevada County after arrival of the 49ers. Located in the churchyard of the first Methodist Church that once occupied this site, it has been in use since 1851. A wooden marker was placed in 1949. The plaque is on a stone monument in the front part of the cemetery. Many famous pioneers and early settlers are buried here, including Henry Meredith and William Alphonso Sutter, the son of General John Sutter. Aaron A. Sargent's cenotaph is here though his remains were cremated and scattered by air above the Quaker Hill Mine, which he used to own.
address: East side of W. Broad St, near Hwy 49

image.alt: Pioneer Cemetery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (I)
description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County and to the Valley of the Sacramento divided on Harmony Ridge near the site of Lone Grave. One route circled the north horizon to Blue Tent, down to Selby Flat, up through this gap and on south.
address: On Airport Rd about 100 feet from junction with Lake Vera Rd

image.alt: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (I), historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Pioneer Emigrant Trail (II)
description: A branch of the Pioneer Emigrant Trail leading from Truckee Pass to the gold mines of Nevada County traversed Harmony Ridge down through this gap and on to the Valley of the Sacramento.
address: 10867 Coyote St, near North Bloomfield Rd

description: Plaque dedicated 1929 by Native Daughters of the Golden West: In memory of our pioneer mothers & fathers dedicated June 12, 1929 by Manzanita Parlor No 29 N.D.G.W.

name: Plaza Grocery
description: This store, opened in 1882 by Tom Shurtleff, contained the only water-powered elevator in Nevada County. Longest continuous operation of any Nevada City grocery until it closed in 1973. Note: Building was a boarding house from 1880 to 1882. Celio family bought it in 1910 and leased to Scheemers in 1928. John Sbaffi bought the business in 1949 and purchased the building in 1965. It was a grocery until 1973, when 'The Jacks' leased it for use as a restaurant. It has been used primarily as a bar or restaurant since.

image.alt: Plaza Grocery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Powell House
description: Built in 1855 as the edifice of the Baptist Church. It was here that famed soprano Emma Nevada (Emma Wixom Palmer) made her debut at the age of three. In 1886 the church was remodeled as the residence of the E. T. R. Powell family. Note: The building now houses apartments.
address: Southeast corner Pine and Spring Sts

image.alt: Powell House, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Preston and Louisa Alexander Homesite
description: Preston Alexander, one of Nevada City's earliest African American settlers, purchased this property along Buckeye Ravine on May 19, 1859. The property was occupied by Preston (1834-1889), his wife, Louisa (1838-1902), and their five children, John, Mary Amelia, Horatio Henry, Louisa, and Rosella Elizabeth, until 1902. The Alexander family was well respected and highly regarded in the community. They were members of the African Methodist Episcopal (A.M.E.) Churches in Nevada City and Grass Valley. Preston Alexander was active in the major civil rights movements of the mid-19th century: abolishing slavery nationally, winning the franchise for African American men, gaining the right for African Americans to testify in court, and securing their access to public accommodations. Alexander Street is named in their honor."

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for Preston and Louisa Alexander Homesite

name: Red Ledge Stamp Mill
description: Stamp Mill. This three stamp mill was donated to the City of Grass Valley by Mrs. Stella Tracy, owner of the famous Red Ledge Mine near Washington, Nevada County, where this mill crushed tons of gold bearing ore for many years. It is erected here in memory of the 'Cousin Jack' miners who came from Cornwall, England with their knowledge of hardrock mining used to bring gold bearing quartz to the surface from the deep mines of the area.
address: Corner of Main and Auburn Sts

image.alt: Red Ledge Stamp Mill, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Reliance Hose Company No. 3
description: This Building, Housing Reliance Hose Company No. 3 since 1891, is hereby marked for preservation, thus honoring The Volunteer Fire Department of Grass Valley March 26, 1988 100th Anniversary of Manzanita Parlor #29, Native Daughters of the Golden West. Note: Marion Leech Conway's memoirs indicate the building was dedicated in December 1911, after housing the company for twenty years. "The doors of Reliance No. 3 have had the latch string out in welcome greeting to all when the word went out the Reliance would entertain its friends."
address: Race St near S. Auburn St

image.alt: Reliance Hose Company No. 3, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: Saint Canice Catholic Church
description: Historic St. Canice Catholic Church was built and first services held in 1864 under the guidance of Father John Griffin. Note: This also is site of St. Peter and St. Paul Catholic Church, built in 1857 and destroyed by fire in 1863.

image.alt: Saint Canice Catholic Church, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: David Nguyen, G Tour Photos, via Google

name: Searls Law Office
description: This building completed December 18, 1872, was the law office for three generations of this family. Niles Searls, who arrived in 1851, practiced law here from 1872 to 1885. He had been elected district attorney in 1855, then district judge for Nevada, Sierra and Yuba counties, then became Chief Justice of the California Supreme Court in 1887. Fred Searls, his son, held the longest tenure in this building (1879-1929). He drafted the first incorporation papers of the hydroelectric industry of California. Carroll Searls, son of Fred, practiced here from 1915 to 1923; later became general counsel of the Newmont Mining Company in New York City; used this office until November 4,1970. Note: Niles Searls was elected district attorney in 1852, not 1855, but because he left California and went east to marry before his term of office began, William M. Stewart was appointed to fill the vacancy. Later, when Stewart resigned in 1854 to become the acting state attorney general, Searls was appointed to replace him as district attorney. From 1972 until 2014 the building housed the Historical SocietyÃ

image.alt: Searls Law Office, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Unknown photographer, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: Shaar Zedek Grass Valley Jewish Cemetery
description: Shaar Zedek, or the Gate of Righteousness, was established in 1856. The first burial was in 1857 and the last in 1891. Thirty headstones are visible. The cemetery was restored around 50 years ago by the Commission for the Preservation of Pioneer Jewish Cemeteries and Landmarks. It is locked and fenced to protect against vandalism.
address: Location: Second Street off Eureka Street

image.alt: Shaar Zedek Grass Valley Jewish Cemetery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: St Patrick's Cemetery
description: St. Patrick's Cemetery 1853. In memory of the pioneers buried here. A sign nearby contains this information: Site of the first Catholic Church in Grass Valley. Dedicated on October 25,1853. Rev. John Shanahan, 1st Pastor. Served as church 'till December 5, 1858. Served as the first Catholic School (1859-1879).

image.alt: St Patrick's Cemetery, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: The Auditorium Building
description: The Auditorium Building. On July 30, 1896, a fire started here at the site of Argall's Opera House, owned by Charles E. Clinch, burning the entire block on East Mill Street from Neal to Bank. In 1900, an incorporation composed of the Native Sons of the Golden West, Ancient Order of the Foresters, Knights of Pythias, Red Men, and the Miner's Union was formed to build a fine theater of three stories. The first floor held a magnificent theater with a capacity of 600 on the floor and 250 in the gallery and office space for the Grass Valley City Hall. The second story was devoted to lodge rooms and the third story was a banquet hall. The basement housed both the kitchen and the armory for Company 1 of the National Guard. On February 2, 1901, the Auditorium Theater opened to a week-long celebration and operated until November 12, 1922. Two days later, it reopened as the Strand Movie Theater, operating until April 22, 1942. The building was then home of the F. S. Rasco & Co. store and since 1972 has been home to Hedman/Ashley furniture store.

image.alt: The Auditorium Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: The Flagg Building
description: The Flagg Bldg. The Eternal Saloon. Built in 1856 on the site of the U.S. Hotel, this brick edifice has served Nevada City as the council chamber, Schreiber's, and since 1957 Eddie Furano's Bank Club. Here at Schreiber's Felix, the marvelous, mysterious music box thundered Strauss waltzes and 'Carmen' to generations with custom and good cheer.

image.alt: The Flagg Building, historic landmark in Nevada County

name: The Old Post Office
description: This building Is known as the Old Post Office. It was built in 1914 by the United States Government as the Grass Valley Post Office. From 1914 until 1984 the Post Office functioned for mail, package and gold shipments from the many mines in the area. After the Post Office moved to East Main Street the building operated as an Insurance Agency in the 1980's and as professional offices since October 1989. Note: Plaque was dedicated in memory of Nevada County pioneer United States Senators Aaron Augustus Sargent (1873-1879) and William Morris Stewart (1865-1875 and 1887-1905).

image.alt: The Old Post Office, historic landmark in Nevada County
image.credit: Unknown authorUnknown author or not provided, via Wikimedia Commons (Public domain)

name: The Red Castle
description: Nevada City's earliest surviving brick home, built in 1860 by Judge John Williams, mine owner, businessman and civic leader, who with his family, crossed the plains in 1849. Near ruin, this house was restored by James Wade Schaar in 1963. The Red Castle stands today as a proud reminder of the part the Argonauts played in the Heritage of California.

image.alt: Editorial placeholder image for The Red Castle
image.originalAlt: The Red Castle in

name: The Union Newspaper
description: Of the many historic newspapers started in the Northern Mines of the great Mother Lode during the famous gold rush era, only The Union of Grass Valley and Nevada City emerged a successful daily published for 100 years. Born in critical times: Civil War and presidential election. The Union has ever strived for unity and has evolved an independent voice supporting man and measure over partisanship. The Union is only the seventh daily newspaper in California to celebrate its Centennial under the original name. Centennial Date, October 28, 1964. Plaque dedicated when The Union newspaper was located on Mill Street, Grass Valley and moved with the newspaper to Sutton Way in December 1978.
address: 11464 Sutton Way, Grass Valley, in the Glenbrook Basin

image.alt: The Union Newspaper in Grass Valley

name: The Willo Steakhouse
description: “In 1947, Bill Davis, a retired U.S. Navy Lt. Commander, purchased and reassembled a surplus WW II Quonset hut from the Navy and opened Bill Davis's Hut, a popular watering hole for the lumber and mining workforce. In 1950, Wilma Kenny leased the property and renamed it Wilma's Hut. Since then, the business was operated by a variety of individuals. In 1963, Bob and Peggy Tucker purchased the hut and renamed it Tuck's Hut. Then in 1969 the Tuckers leased the property to Frank Williams, owner of the Willo bar in Grass Valley, who was displaced by the Gold Country Freeway construction. He partnered with Veda Folden who, with the help of her husband and brothers, converted the covered structure into the main dining room. The Willo steakhouse was born. Many folks through the decades have asked why the Willo has no 'w' on the end (Willow). The Willo is a combination of Frank Williams last name and his wife's first name Lola. In 1976 Ken and Jeannie Hiebert purchased the property from Peggy Tucker and combined the bar and restaurant operations. They operated it for 26 years before selling it to the current owners, Mike Byrne and Nancy Wilson who at the time of this dedication are committed to preserving the California roadhouse heritage of the Willo, which has served over 1,000,000 choice New York steaks."

image.alt: The Willo Steakhouse in Nevada City

name: Trinity Episcopal Church
description: Trinity Episcopal Church. Founded as a parish in 1855, opened for Divine Service in this building on November 2, 1873. Previously on this site was â€˜Caldwell's Upper Store' built in 1849 for the miners of Upper Deer Creek Dry Diggings, now known as Nevada City, and Washington School, 1863, the community's first high school. The present building was erected by shipwrights working in the mines. Their craftsmanship may be seen in its construction. Notes: CaldwellÃ
address: Corner of Nevada and High Sts

image.alt: Trinity Episcopal Church, historic landmark in Nevada County
